coredata.dc:title: Heterogeneous synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots prepared via anhydrous citric acid and melamine for selective and sensitive turn on-off-on detection of Hg (II), glutathione and its cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Iqbal, Anam
Iqbal, Kanwal
Xu, Lige
Li, Bo
Gong, Deyan
Liu, Xiaoyu
Guo, Yali
Liu, Weisheng
Qin, Wenwu
Guo, Huichen
coredata.dc:description: Herein a new kind of convenient, low-cost, environmentally friendly and high-output heterogeneous one step synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was developed by reacting anhydrous citric acid used as the carbon source and melamine as the surface passivation for nitrogen source without the use of any acid, alkali, organic solvents or metal ions along with high quantum yield (40%±0.06) by solid state method. The emission spectrum shows a strong peak at 440nm when excited at 360nm, in the detection only the Hg (II) can readily quench the Photoluminescence (PL) of the N-CDs due to the strong electrostatic interaction and electron transfer between N-CDs and Hg (II), upon the addition of GSH PL of the N-CDs can be recovered owing to the preferred combination of Hg (II) and GSH by forming the Hg (II)-S or Hg (II)-NH bond indicating a remarkably high selectivity towards Hg2+ and biothiols (especially for GSH) among other metals and amino acids with a detection limit as low as 20nM and 40nM. With no apparent cytotoxicity, N-CDs could enter the baby hamster kidney (BHK) cells, indicating a practical potential probe for cellular imaging and labeling. Endowed the resulting N-CDs possess excitation dependent PL properties with relatively high sensitivity and selectivity, for the detection of Hg (II) and GSH in aqueous media, environmental and biological sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CDs
On-off-on
Sensor
Hg (II)
Glutathione
Fluorescence cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–vis absorption and steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy
Selectivity and quantitative analysis for Hg2+ and GSH
Cell culture, fluorescence imaging and cytotoxicity assay
Synthesis and characterization of N-CDs (melamine+anhydrous citric acid)
PL turn-off of N-CDs in the presence of Hg (II)
PL restoration of N-CDs-Hg (II) in the presence of GSH
Selectivity of the N-CDs-Hg (II) system and interference measurements

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Representative TEM (A, B) and HRTEM (C) images of as prepared N-CDs. The crystalline lattice is identified in C. (D) Size distribution histogram of N-CDs.
Photoluminescent emission spectra (excitation wavelength (λex) from 280 to 480nm) of N-CDs in HEPES (12mM) buffer at pH 7.4.

Photoluminescent spectra of N-CDs in the presence of different amounts of Hg2+ in HEPES (12mM) buffer at pH 7.4. Hg2+concentration from top to bottom: (0, 0.4, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56, 64, 72, 80, 92, 104, 120, 140, 160, and 200μM) (λex =360nm).
Photoluminescent response of N-CDs (120μg/mL) in the presence of different metals (700μM), Hg2+ (70μM) in HEPES (12mM) buffer at pH 7.4. (λex =360nm).

Photoluminescent spectra of N- CDs-Hg (II) (100μM) in the presence of different amounts of GSH in HEPES (12mM) buffer at pH 7.4. GSH concentration from bottom to top: (0, 0.8, 2, 3.2, 6, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56, 68, 80, 96, 116, 136, 160, 184, 224 and 344μM) (λex =360nm).
Photoluminescent response of N-CDs-Hg in the presence of different amino acids and anions (HEPES (12mM) buffer at pH 7.4.), (λex =360nm). All the concentration of the amino acids and anions is 500μM.
Schematic representation of formation of N-CDs, the binding of N-CDs with Hg2+ and GSH and the quenching off-on N-CDs by Hg2+and GSH.

Confocal fluorescence images of live BHK-cells. (1) fluorescence image of BHK-cells incubated with N-CDs for 1h at 37°C. (2) fluorescence image of N-CDs-stained BHK-cells exposed to 100μM Hg2+ for 30min. (3) fluorescence image of N-CDs-Hg(II)-stained BHK-cells exposed to 344μM GSH for 30min (scale bar 25μM).
Comparison of fluorescence N-CDs probes for Hg2+/ GSH based fluorescence quencher.
Comparison of fluorescence N-CDs probes for Hg2+ based fluorescence quencher.

head.ce:title: Heterogeneous synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots prepared via anhydrous citric acid and melamine for selective and sensitive turn on-off-on detection of Hg (II), glutathione and its cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province and State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province and State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Veterinary Etiological Biology and Key Laboratory of Animal Virology of Ministry of Agriculture, Lanzhou Veterinary Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Xujiaping 1, Lanzhou, Gansu Province 730046, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Veterinary Etiological Biology and Key Laboratory of Animal Virology of Ministry of Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qinghai Institute of Salt Lakes, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining, Qinghai 810008, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Petrochemical Technology, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou,730050, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The N-CDs could be potentially utilized as an excellent optical sensor for the quantitative analysis of Hg (II) or GSH in living cells.

Herein a new kind of convenient, low-cost, environmentally friendly and high-output heterogeneous one step synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was developed by reacting anhydrous citric acid used as the carbon source and melamine as the surface passivation for nitrogen source without the use of any acid, alkali, organic solvents or metal ions along with high quantum yield (40%±0.06) by solid state method. The emission spectrum shows a strong peak at 440nm when excited at 360nm, in the detection only the Hg (II) can readily quench the Photoluminescence (PL) of the N-CDs due to the strong electrostatic interaction and electron transfer between N-CDs and Hg (II), upon the addition of GSH PL of the N-CDs can be recovered owing to the preferred combination of Hg (II) and GSH by forming the Hg (II)-S or Hg (II)-NH bond indicating a remarkably high selectivity towards Hg2+ and biothiols (especially for GSH) among other metals and amino acids with a detection limit as low as 20nM and 40nM. With no apparent cytotoxicity, N-CDs could enter the baby hamster kidney (BHK) cells, indicating a practical potential probe for cellular imaging and labeling. Endowed the resulting N-CDs possess excitation dependent PL properties with relatively high sensitivity and selectivity, for the detection of Hg (II) and GSH in aqueous media, environmental and biological sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Small-molecule reactive species plays a crucial role in cell signaling and thus are involved in both human health and diseases. Consequently, the detection of these biomolecules is of immense importance in living cell and in vivo. Biothiols, such as glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), and homocysteine (Hcy) are family of biomolecules with thiol group that exists in living systems have received greatest attention owing to the fact, that playing the significant role in maintaining redox balance of various physiological processes that are crucial for maintaining life owing to an equilibrium that exists in biological systems between their reduced free thiol and oxidized disulfide forms [1]. As a result, biothiols act as an antioxidant to maintain the normal levels of peroxy radicals, aid in the inhibition of cellular apoptosis, and participate in the construction of complicated multi-dimensional protein synthesis, while abnormal concentration of these species are considered to be signals for many diseases such as cancer, AIDS, osteoporosis, Alzheimer, liver damage, heart, inflammatory bowel, neurotoxicity and cardiovascular disease [2]. Owing to these features, the development of simple and low-cost strategy for selective and sensitive detection of biothiols in biological sample in the area of early clinical diagnosis of these diseases is highly desirable.

Recently, great attention has been paid to PL probes for detecting biothiols, including (GSH), (Cys), and (Hcy), owing to the advantages of high sensitivity, specificity, and real-time determination [2,3]. Han et al. [4] used CdTe quantum dots as probes for PL detection of biothiols based upon the competition effect between thymidine-thymidine mismatch and biothiols with Hg (II). Similar works have been reported in the recent past for various PL Probe materials, such as conjugated polymers [5] noble metal nano materials [6,7] PL molecular probes [8,9] colloid semiconductor quantum dots [4].

Meanwhile, Hg (II) is one of the most prevalent toxic metal in environment and health concern, which can enrich in the form of food chain and absorbed by human, because it can easily pass through the biological membranes (i.e., skin, respiratory and gastrointestinal tissue), and even destroy the human body. Therefore, the detection and quantification of Hg (II) is of an immense importance. Thus, it is very important to develop an effective method for sensitive detection of trace amount of Hg (II). Conventionally, different analytical methods such as, atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy, auger-electron spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, and polarography are usually employed for detection of heavy metal ions, owing to their efficiency and sensitivity. However, the above methods usually require complicated instrumentation or time-consuming sample treatment, which severely limit their practical and commercial applications. Alternatively, nanoparticle-based PL assay of Hg(II) has been developed, because of its high sensitivity, fast analysis, and non-sample-destructing. Generally, Au nanoparticles [10], Ag nanoparticles [11], CdTe quantum dots [4] and CDs have been reported as novel and efficient probe for the detection of Hg (II) [12–14] as PL probes.

Among these reported PL probes, carbon dots (CDs) have recently drawn an immense attention owing to their unique properties such as high water solubility, good optical properties along with chemical inertness, excellent cell permeability, superiority in photo stability, non-blinking fluorescence emission, excitation-dependent emission tunability, flexibility in surface functionalization, easiness of preparation, remarkably low cytotoxicity, environmentally friendliness and an excellent biocompatibility [15,16].

Recently, the fluorescence of CDs was observed to be turned-off in the presence of metal ions, and restored after the formation of complexes between the metal ion and the chelators [17]. Based on this mechanism, a lot of PL “off-on” sensors developed from CDs for the detection of chelators, most of them are important in biology. For instance, It was demonstrated that the “off” PL of CDs could be achieved by using Hg (II) and the “on” PL was realized with I− [14]. The quenching of CDs PL using Cu2+ was reported and the restoration of PL was observed after the introduction of l-cysteine (L-Cys) [18]. However, to produce CDs with adequate quantum yields (QYs) of PL, a surface-passivating procedure is necessary [19].

Different methods have been carried out to improve the properties of CDs, such as surface passivation and chemical doping. In order to enrich the potential applications, the doping of other elements into carbon nanostructures is the efficient way to tune the electronic structures. So chemical doping with heteroatoms such as boron or nitrogen atoms is an effective strategy to modulate the electronic properties and surface chemistry for carbon materials [20] for instance, nitrogen atoms can be easily doped into carbon structures because it is well known that N, a neighbor to the C atom in the periodic table, is a general dopant within the carbon materials via forming CN bonds due to the similarity of atomic size and five-valence-electron structure can inject electrons into carbon-based materials thus changing the electronic and transport properties. The previous studies has showed that the functional group containing N, and O on CDs may improve their ability of chelation with metal ions, leading to an efficient quenching [12,21–23] and then can be recovered by chelators. For these above-mentioned reasons melamine (a nitrogen-rich organic compound used as the surface passivating reagent as the nitrogen source) is the best choice to functionalize the CDs. N-CDs have been successfully prepared in the presence of nitrogen containing species (i.e. NH3 and pyridine). Moreover, as Hg (II) has strong interaction with thiol functional group, the “off” PL of N-CDs by Hg (II) could be recovered by biothiol and served as an “on-off-on” PL probe for the detection of Hg (II) and biothiols.

Herein in this work we present a new kind of convenient, low-cost, environmentally friendly, and high-output heterogeneous one-step new kind of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs).The as synthesized N-CDs emit bright blue-luminescence with 40%±0.06 quantum yield, with well-defined surface chemistry and properties could be well-dispersed in an aqueous solution and display an excitation tunable luminescence as well as high photostability, was developed by reacting anhydrous citric acid was used as the carbon source and melamine is used as a surface passivating reagent N-CDs with high percentage of nitrogen content [12,20,24–27] without the use of any additives by solid state method for PL turn on-off-on probe for Hg(II) and GSH by tuning their surface structures and the related PL properties. This platform was simple, convenient, low-cost, environmentally friendly and selective, which was applied to detect GSH in BHK-cells.

Anhydrous citric acid was purchased from Guangfu Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Melamine (99%) was purchased from Sinopharm chemical Regent Co. Ltd (Beijing, China). The chlorides of (Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and NH4 +) sulfate of Fe2+, acetate of Zn2+ and nitrates of Hg2+ and Ag+ anions of sodium (S2−and I−) were purchased from Heowns (Tianjing, China). Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), reduced l-glutathione (GSH), d-aspartic acid (Asp), l-cysteine (Cys), l-glutamic Acid (Glu), ß-alanine (Ala), l-arginine (Arg), glycine (Gly), l-lysine (Lys), l-asparagine (Asn), l-glutamine (Gln), l-histidine (Hls), l-leucine (Leu), l-isoleucine (lle), l-methionine (Met), l-phenylalanine (Phe), l-proline (Pro), l-serine (Ser), l-threonine (Thr), l-tryptophan (Trp), l-tyrosine (Tyr) and l-valine (Val) were purchased from Heowns (Tianjing, China), All reagents are of analytical reagent grade. Ultrapure water obtained from a Milli-Q ultrapure (18.2MΩcm) system was used in all experiments.

XRD measurement was performed on X-ray diffractometer (Philips X’Pert Holland) with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54059), with opening voltage and current at 40kV and 35mA. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy at an accelerating voltage of 200Kev. Dynamic light scattered (DLS) was got on a BI-200SM (USA Brookhaven) at room temperature. The Fourier transform infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were measured by Nicolet 360 FTIR spectrometer with the KBr pellet technique. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was measured on a PHI-550 spectrometer by using Mg Ka radiation (hv=1253.6) photoemission spectroscopy with a base vacuum operated at 300W. Elemental analysis for N-CDs was analyzed by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) (Thermo Jarrel Ash,Franklin, MA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis absorption and steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Varian UV-Cary100 spectrophotometer, and for the corrected steady-state fluorescence emission spectra, a FLS920 spectrofluorometer was employed. PL quantum yields were determined by comparison of the integral of the emission bands with the one of quinine sulfate/0.5M H2SO4 [Φ =0.54] [28]. PL lifetimes were measured on an Edinburgh Instruments FLS920 equipped with the light emitting diodes (excitation wavelength 360nm), using the time-correlated single photon counting technique in 2048 channels at room temperature.

Histograms of the instrument response functions (using a LUDOX scatter) and sample decays were recorded until they typically reached 1.0×104 counts in the peak channel. Obtained histograms were fitted as sums of the exponentials, using Gaussian-weighted nonlinear least squares fitting based on Marquardt-Levenberg minimization implemented in the software package of the instrument. The fitting parameters (decay times and preexponential factors) were determined by minimizing the reduced chi-square χ2. An additional graphical method was used to judge the quality of the fit that included plots of surfaces (“carpets”) of the weighted residuals vs channel number. All curve fittings presented here had χ2 values <1.

Photoluminescent N-CDs were prepared by solid state method of anhydrous citric acid and melamine without any additives (acids, alkali, organic solvents or metal ions). The as prepared N-CDs was checked with different ratio of anhydrous citric acid and melamine but the best ratio for selectivity and sensitivity is 1:2, see Table SI.1. In a typical synthesis 0.39g of anhydrous citric acid and 0.80g melamine were transferred into a 50mL Teflon lined autoclave without any additives (acids, alkali, organic solvents or metal ions) by solid state method and heated at 180°C for 5h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The solid greenish-black photoluminescent N-CDs was obtained. The product was transferred to 100mL nano pure water and centrifuge at 10,000rpm for 15min to remove the large and unreacted product, the concentration and pH of the N-CDs aqueous solution are about (120μg/mL) and 7.4 respectively. Finally, the supernatant of N-CDs was dialyzed overnight using dialysis membranes of 1000 cut off, diluted to 100.00mL HEPES (12.5mM) as stock solution for further characterization and use.
As a control, the CDs-CA was also prepared by the same manner without the addition of melamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and quantitative analysis for Hg2+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Hg2+ and GSH was performed at room temperature in an aqueous solution of N-CDs at pH 7.4 using HEPES (12.5mM) excited at 360nm. High selectivity towards the analyte is an important property of N-CDs. To evaluate the sensitivity towards Hg2+ different concentration of 0.001M Hg2+ (pH 7.4) were added into the aqueous solution of N-CDs (2500μL) and the mixed solution was sonicated for 1min before spectral measurements and the final concentration of Hg2+ was in the range of (0–200μM) achieved at an excitation wavelength 360nm. The similar procedure was performed for the detection of other metal ions. To evaluate the sensitivity of GSH towards N-CDs-Hg(II), 100μM of 0.001M Hg2+ (pH 7.4) was added into the aqueous solution of N-CDs-Hg(II) (2500μL) and the mixed solution was sonicated for 1min before spectral measurements and the final concentration of GSH was in the range of (0–344μM) achieved at an excitation wavelength 360nm. The similar procedure was performed for the detection of other amino acids and anions The PL spectra were operated by operating the fluorescence spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 360nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture, fluorescence imaging and cytotoxicity assay
ce:section.ce:para: Baby hamster kidney cells (BHK) were obtained from the cell bank of shanghai science Academy. The cells were seeded in culture dishes containing DMEM medium with 10%, calf serum, 100 unit’s mL−1 penicillin, 100μgmL−1streptomycin and 100μgmL−1 neomycin, incubated in a fully humidified incubator at 37°C with 5% CO2 when the cells reached 80% confluence with normal morphology, these cells were ready for further experiments.

After removal of the culture medium, cells were incubated with N-CDs in 1.0mL of fresh culture medium for 2h. Before imaging measurement, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove the residual N-CDs. To observe fluorescence changes of the medium, 100μM HgNO3·5H2O and GSH 344μM was added into the N-CDs and N-CDs-Hg (II) loaded cells and then further incubated for 0.5h at 37°C. The change of fluorescence was observed using a confocal microscope and the excitation wavelength of confocal microscope was 360nm. Cells were observed under an Olympus FV1000-IX81 laser confocal microscope.

The commercial kit (CCK-8 Kit) has been employed for the cytotoxicity assay. BHK Cells were seeded in 96-well assay plates at a density of 104 cells per well (100μL total volume/well) for 24h. The as-prepared N-CDs, (0μgmL−1, 10μgmL−1, 20μgmL−1, 40μgmL−1, 80μgmL−1 and 100μgmL−1) were added in the serum-free medium and incubated with the cells for 24h after that. The cells were cultivated in serum-free medium with 10% CCK-8 solution for 2h after washed with PBS for three times. The optical absorbance of the cells was detected at 450nm through a microplate reader (German Berthold Mithras 2LB943). The control experiment was finished by detecting the growth culture medium without N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N-CDs (melamine+anhydrous citric acid)
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent N-CDs in this synthesis were prepared by the one step solid state synthesis of anhydrous citric acid and melamine without any additives at 180°C leads to a greenish-black solid soluble in water give yellowish dispersion. Typically, nitrogen doping agent melamine respectively reacted with anhydrous citric acid under solid state condition. After the intermolecular and intramolecular and polymerization among the −COOH, −OH, and −NH2 groups, the activated intermediates were carbonized to form the aromatic carbonic rings. N atoms were simultaneously incorporated as pyridine, pyrrolyl, and amide moieties into heterocyclic ring system and such precursors were further condensed to form the final N-CDs [29]. The formation of N-CDs was confirmed by TEM measurements. As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, the resulting N-CDs appear as spherical particles with good monodispersity. The size distribution in Fig. 1D, shows that the average hydrodynamic diameter of N-CDs has a size less than 3nm, obtained from DLS measurements, which is good accordance with the size of CDs observed previously. The crystalline nature of N-CDs was then examined by High-resolution (HRTEM) measurements as shown in Fig. 1C, shows the high crystallinity with the lattice spacing of 0.21nm and 0.28nm, which is close to the (002) facet of graphite and the diffraction planes (102) of sp2 graphitic carbon and diffraction plane (103) of diamond-like carbon.

To study the crystallinity of solid phase N-CDs, XRD investigation was performed The XRD patterns of the N-CDs displayed a broad peak centered at 2θ=20° implying the amorphous nature of N-CDs, which consist of highly disordered carbon core with an amorphous surface structure as shown in Fig. S1

To investigate the composition and functional group of N-CDs, FTIR analysis was performed. Fig. S2 presents the FTIR spectrum of N-CDs. With respect to N-CDs, the typical absorption band at 3100–3500cm−1 are assigned to (OH) and (NH) stretching vibration. These bands suggest that hydroxyl and amide groups are the main passivating groups on the surface of N-CDs. These functional groups improve the hydrophilicity and stability of N-CDs in aqueous system. Absorption bands from 2500 to 3150cm−1 are assigned to (CH) of aromatic stretching. The absorption band at 1600–1700cm−1 are assigned to (CO) from stretching vibration from (COOH) group. The peak at 1714cm−1 is primarily due to the amide linkage (-CONH). Absorption band at 1552 and1411cm−1 are assigned to 1,3,5-s-triazine ring (residue of melamine). The absorption band at 1200cm−1 are assigned to CN respectively. These results shows that the increased amount of doping molecule enhanced NH and CN intensities can be attributed to an increasing amount of amide bond formation, where the amine group of melamine is progressively reacted with residual carboxyl group of citric acid.

The chemical composition and surface state of N-CDs at selected doping amount was examined by X-ray photo electron spectroscopy (XPS) Fig. S3. N-CDs have three peaks centered at 286, 398, and 532eV, which correspond to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s respectively. The XPS spectra clearly demonstrate that nitrogen atom has been successfully doped into the CDs. The component ratio analysis for N-CDs demonstrate that 31.74% of carbon, 54.98% of nitrogen and 20.83% of oxygen consist in N-CDs Table S2, this result reveals that N-CDs is nitrogen rich doped carbon dot completed through an adequate carbonization which is the highest percentage of nitrogen content in reported before with other organic quantum dots, methods and precursors [16,19–22]. Such result reveals that the N-CDs with high nitrogen doping concentration of nitrogen can be synthesized by using melamine as nitrogen containing precursor.

High-resolution (HR-XPS) was applied to give further insight into the molecular state and bonding manner of those elements in N-CDs. The C 1s, O 1s and N 1s spectra were fitted into Gaussian peaks at 284.47 (CC/CC), 285.39 (COH), 287.31 (CO) and 288.38 (CO)eV for C1s, 530.52 (COH), 531.54 (CO/COC) and 533.03 (OCO*)eV for O1s, 397.84 (CN), 398.64 (CN) and 399.35 (CN) eV for N1s [30]. It reveals that the presence of sp2/sp3 carbon (CC/CC) is associated with the carbonic core in CDs, where the appearance of COH, CO/COC, OCO*, CN, CN, and CN correspond to the oxygen-containing and N-doping groups dominated on the surface of CDs. Furthermore, the peak intensities of CN, −CN, and CN at 397.84, 398.64 and 399.35eV are correspondingly correlated to the PL QY of the CDs.

UV/Vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs in HEPES (12mM) buffer at pH 7.4 is depicted in Fig. S4. The absorption spectra are of similar shape as those of described CDs [31,32] N-CDs shows broad absorption bands with absorption maxima at 335nm which attributable to the π-π* transition of CO/COH/CC/CN bonds and shoulder band at around 383 and 413nm associated with the surface passivation state of N-CDs.

It has been reported that the fluorescence emission spectra and PL intensity of N-CDs depend on the excitation wavelength. To further investigate the optical properties of the N-CDs a series of emission spectra for the N-CDs were recorded at different excitation wavelength ranging from 280 to 480nm, as shown in Fig. 2 . The maximum excitation wavelength of N-CDs is observed at 360nm. The emission spectrum shows a strong peak at 440nm when excited at 360nm. The blue PL of N-CDs in an aqueous solution observed at 365nm UV-light can be seen by naked eye (as shown in Fig. S5). When an excited wavelength changed from 280 to 480nm, the emission peak shows red shift. It shows that the N-CDs exhibit an excitation dependent PL behavior, which is similar to other luminescent carbon nanoparticles. The N-CDs is very stable because its PL is almost unchanged after keeping it for 6-month under room temp. This excellent stability makes the N-CDs as PL probe. Using quinine sulfate (quantum yield=54%) as a standard, the quantum yield of the present N-CDs was determined to be 40%±0.06.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL turn-off of N-CDs in the presence of Hg (II)
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in Figs. 3 and S6, the PL of the N-CDs could be effectively quenched by Hg2+ due to the strong electrostatic interaction and electron transfer between N-CDs and Hg (II), because the as prepared N-CDs contains ample of functional groups, such as −OH, −NH2, −COOH, and −CONH on the surface of the N-CDs could generate complexes with Hg2+ because of the synergistic effect, so the PL intensity of the N-CDs could be efficiently quenched by Hg (II) making it possible to detect Hg2+ over other metal ions. Other metal ions i.e. Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, NH4 +, Zn2+, Hg2+ and Ag+ in the 700μM concentration range to solution of N-CDs in HEPES buffer solution, had no obvious effect on the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The high selectivity of N-CDs towards Hg (II) suggests that it is possible to apply the N-CDs for sensing Hg (II). As plotted in Fig. 3, the PL intensity of N-CDs significantly decreased with the increase of Hg2+ concentration while upon further increasing the Hg (II) concentration, a significant decrease of the PL intensity and PL quantum yield is observed with no other spectral changes (i.e. no spectral shifts or formation of new emission bands). The PL emission spectra of N-CDs are shown as a function of [Hg (II)] in Fig. 3, the (N-CDs-Hg (II)) nanocomposite could accelerate the excitation transfer and regulate the charge recombination, resulting in an obvious PL quenching was also investigated. When the concentration of Hg (II) reached 200μΜ at 360nm, the PL was quenched by 98%. The PL quantum yields of N-CDs without and with high concentration of Hg2+ was found to be 0.01, (at [Hg (II)]=200μM).

Moreover, the PL intensity decreased almost linearly with increasing concentration of Hg (II) (0–24μM) at 360nm in HEPES buffer at pH 7.4. The relationship between the concentration of Hg (II) and the quenched PL could be adopted for quantitative analysis of Hg (II) as there is a good correlation between the quenching efficiency (F) and the concentration of Hg (II) in the range from 0 to 24μM at 360nm of Hg (II) as shown in Fig. S7.The calibration curve for concentration of Hg (II) in HEPES buffer at pH 7.4 was obtained as shown Fig. S7, where F stands for the fluorescence peak intensity in HEPES buffer at pH 7.4 upon addition of Hg (II).
F=4087–052.50×[Hg2+] (r=0.99925 [Hg2+]=0–24μM)
The calibration equation serves as the quantitative basis for the detection of Hg (II) in aqueous solution.
UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs dissolved in HEPES buffer solution is analyze for Hg (II) and biothiols the UV–vis spectrum shown in Fig. S5.

The detection limit (DL) was estimated based on the following equation:
DL=3.3×σ/k
Where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample and k is the slope of the calibration curve. The DL of Hg2+ was 20nM at 360nm in aqueous solution.

To explore the specificity of PL quenched by Hg2+ the alone anhydrous citric acid CDs was prepared by similar method doesn’t show any selectivity for any metal ions (alkali, alkaline-earth, and transition metal ions) including Hg2+ Fig. S14. The response of N-CDs was examined upon addition of 700μM by using some interfering metals such as (Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn+2, Ag+, Hg2+ and NH4 +) under the identical conditions of Hg2+ (70μM) at 360nm excitation. As shown in Figs. 4 and S.6. Only Hg2+ induced a prominent intensity reduction. These results confirmed that N-CDs shows an excellent hypersensitivity and hyperselectivity towards Hg2+.Therefore, this selective binding event and the corresponding PL quenching process can offer the N-CDs as a selective turn-off probe for Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL restoration of N-CDs-Hg (II) in the presence of GSH
ce:section.ce:para: Since Hg (II) has a great affinity towards thiol-containing GSH, Cys, and Hcy, their strong coordination could recover the quenched PL emission of N-CDs-Hg (II) system. Such PL turn-on sensing system can be used for sensitive and selective detection of GSH. It can be seen that the PL intensity at once change with the addition of GSH the emission spectrum was collected after 1min. For the original solution, there is very weak emission at 440nm due to the strong quenching effect of Hg (II). Clearly with the addition of GSH, the PL is recovered gradually and the emission intensity increases with increasing the GSH concentration from (0–344μM) at 360nm, when we increased the concentration Hg (II) from 24μM to 224μM at 360nm, the PL was 10–20-fold enhanced after the reaction of probe with 344μM GSH Fig. 5 . Hence, with the presence of GSH, the PL intensity of the N-CDs can be recovered due to the release of Hg (II) from the surface of N-CDs by forming the strong Hg (II)-S bond or Hg (II)-NH bond. Fig. S8 displays good linear relationship between F and the GSH concentration from (0–56μM) at 360nm, where F is the recovered fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs-Hg (II) in the presence of GSH. The detection limit is estimated to be 40nM which is much lower than those of the previously reported fluorescence probe Table S4

Similar work is done with Cys and Hcy Fig. S9a and S10a, but the recovered fluorescence intensity is much lower than GSH Fig. S8. The plausible reason for the selectivity for GSH over Cys and Hcy was that GSH had the multidentate anchor and the specific steric structure, which led to the coordination priority with Hg. These results indicated that the developing sensing system exhibited excellent selectivity so this probe is more sensitive towards GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the N-CDs-Hg (II) system and interference measurements
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the N-CDs-Hg (II) for GSH detection, the PL response to the other amino acids and anions (S2−and I−) at the same concentration were also investigated. Amino acids and anions (S2−and I−) 500μM were added in to the 2500μL of N-CDs-Hg (II). As shown in Fig. 6 no more PL was observed after the addition of amino acids and anions (S2−and I−) which indicated that amino acids and anions (S2−and I−) had no competitive reaction with GSH except Cys and Hcy. The restoration ability follows the order of GSH>Cys>Hcy. Moreover, the PL recovery efficiency of GSH is significantly higher than those of other amino acids and anions. It should be pointed out that although, Cys and Hcy can also turn on the quenched fluorescence, the intracellular level of GSH in mammalian cell is higher than Cys and Hcy. Hence, the N-CDs-Hg (II) could be a promising sensing platform for detecting the intracellular GSH with high selectivity.

PL quenching usually originate from static or dynamic quenching in which the fluorescent molecule reacts with the quencher. Static quenching involves the formation of the complex by combining the ground state Photoluminescent molecule with the quencher while in dynamic quenching the collision of excited Photoluminescent molecule with the quencher. To better explore the quenching mechanism, we also calculated the quenching equation according to the Stern-Volmer equation F 0/F =1+ ks[Q] from Fig. S12, where Ks is the association equilibrium constant, F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity without the quencher (Hg2+), F is the fluorescence intensity after adding the quencher of concentration. As shown in Fig. S12 a good linear Stern-Volmer relationship is observed in (N-CDs)n for Hg2+, Since the presence of Hg2+ and biothiols in the N-CDs aqueous solution does not change the PL lifetime of N-CDs, we may conclude that the quenching process is a static mechanism.

To investigate the PL dynamics of N-CDs,:PL decay traces of N-CDs was recorded at 440nm by the single-photon timing method [33]. PL decay for N-CDs water revealed tri-exponential behavior. The PL decay is fitted to be tri- exponential profile with the lifetime of ∼1.09ns (12.16%), ∼4.84ns (40.50%) and ∼11.53ns (47.34%) in water. Upon addition of 140μM Hg2+ and GSH (344μM), Cys (1020μM) and Hcy (800μM) to aqueous solution of N-CDs, the PL decay time does not change (Fig. 7 ). The measured lifetime in the presence of N-CDs- Hg2+ (τ1 =0.63ns, 19.01%, τ2 =4.04ns, 27.83% and τ3 =12.23ns, 53.16%) or GSH (τ1 =1.01ns, 14.36%, τ2 =4.45ns, 42.47% and τ3 =11.14ns, 43.18%) is the same as the lifetime of the N-CDs (τ1 =1.09ns, 12.16%, τ2 =4.84ns, 40.50% and τ3 =11.53ns, 47.34%) in water. With the further increase of Hg2+ or GSH concentration, the PL decay time does not change either, similar with Cys and Hcy Table S3.

Quenching of PL upon Hg2+ binding arises from an ET (electron transfer) from the N-CDs fluorophore to the metal center. The decrease of the PL intensity with increasing concentration of Hg2+ means that the concentration of non-fluorescent form (complex) increases while that of the PL form (N-CDs) decreases. This non-fluorescent complex species formed on addition of Hg2+ doesn’t change the PL emission maximum or the lifetime.

Carbon dots made from individual anhydrous citric acid CDs (CA) doesn’t shown any selectivity for any metal ions (alkali, alkaline-earth, and transition metal ions) including Hg2+ (Fig. S13). As we mention before, carbon dots are versatile and tuneable surface functionalities due to it have −NH, −OH, −NH2, −COOH, and −CONH groups on its surface. Through surface functionalization, the residue melamine can easily modify on the surface on the citric acid and got the functionalized N-CDs.

In the recent literature, some PL CDs based chemosensors for Hg2+ and GSH ions have been reported. In order to evaluate the evaluate the effectiveness of N-CDs as a potential probe for the determination of Hg2+ and GSH performance of Hg2+ and GSH as PL probe, a comparison of the method with the published work related to PL detection of Hg2+ and GSH based on CDs is listed in Tables 1 and 2. The result indicated that the N-CDs could be potentially utilized as an excellent optical sensor for the quantitative analysis of Hg2+ in aqueous solution.
So, N-CDs have highly selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+ and GSH. Schematic representation of formation of N-CDs and its binding with Hg2+ and GSH are shown in Fig. 7.

For practical applications, it is necessary that the molecular probe described here is able to detect Hg2+ and GSH under biological conditions. Subsequent experiments probed the ability of N-CDs to track GSH levels in living cells using a confocal microscope. BHK cells were incubated with N-CDs and then further incubated with 100μM Hg2+ or 344μM GSH. The cells incubated with N-CDs show blue fluorescence when excited by ultraviolet light (Fig. 8 -1b). From Fig. 8-1b and Fig. S15 it can be concluded that N-CDs efficiently accumulates mainly in the cell membrane and inside the cytosol, but is excluded from the cell nucleus. In contrast, N-CDs stained cells exposed to Hg2+ for 30min at 37°C display negligible intracellular fluorescence (Fig. 8-2b). A bright-field transmission image of cells treated with CDs and Hg2+ confirmed that the cells were viable throughout the imaging experiments (Fig. 8-1a, 2a & 3a) after above cells treated with GSH for another 30min, fluorescence image of the cells revealed a remarkable enhancement of intracellular fluorescence (Fig. 8-2b). These findings are consistent with the steady-state fluorescence experiment results discussed in previous section.

Moreover, cell cytotoxicity experiments of N-CDs were evaluated using BHK-cells lines through the MTS assay. As shown in Fig. S16, the N-CDs exhibited low cytotoxicity, which may be due to the low toxicity of CDs. The result confirmed that the probe N-CDs can be used to image intracellular Hg2+ or GSH level in living cells.

In conclusion, we have synthesized and characterized a highly photoluminescent N-CDs with relatively high sensitivity and selectivity for label-free quantitative detection of Hg (II) and GSH with 40%±0.06 quantum yield prepared by a simple solid state method. This method relies on the fact that Hg (II) is able to quench the PL of the N-CDs, while GSH can effectively recover the PL quenching due to the release of Hg (II) from the surface of N-CDs by forming the strong Hg (II)-S bond or Hg (II)-NH bond. This assay has several notable features, first the assay presented herein is simple, one-step in design and offers a convenient protocol for rapid detection of Hg (II) and GSH verses other metal ions and the other 19 natural amino acids with a low limit of detection 20nM and 40nM respectively. The application of N-CDs can be used for cell imaging and labeling. From these results, it can be concluded that the N-CDs is sensitive, selective, simple and rapid for the determination of trace Hg+2 and GSH. Furthermore, no further chemical modification for N-CDs is required, which offers the advantages of simplicity and cost efficiency. We except that this strategy may offer a novel approach for developing low-cost and sensitive sensors in biological and environmental analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank The Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21771092) and supported by the State Key Laboratory of Veterinary Etiological Biology, Lanzhou Veterinary Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences. This work was supported by The Gansu Province Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 1508RJZA078).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.08.130.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structure, mechanism and regulation of peroxiredoxins
Cyanine-based fluorescent probe for highly selective detection of glutathione in cell cultures and live mouse tissues
BODIPY-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for highly selective detection of glutathione over cysteine and homocysteine
Sensitive and selective sensor for biothiols in the cell based on the recovered fluorescence of the CdTe quantum dots- Hg (II) system
Water-soluble conjugated polymer–Cu (II) system as a turn-on fluorescence probe for label-free detection of glutathione and cysteine in biological fluids
Band-selective coupling-induced enhancement of two-photon photoluminescence in gold nanocubes and its application as turn-on fluorescent probes for cysteine and glutathione
A squaraine-based red emission off–on chemosensor for biothiols and its application in living cells imaging
Chromogenic and fluorogenic sensing of biological thiols in aqueous solutions using BODIPY-based reagents
A disulfide bound-molecular beacon as a fluorescent probe for the detection of reduced glutathione and its application in cells
Microwave-assisted rapid synthesis of luminescent gold nanoclusters for sensing Hg 2+ in living cells using fluorescence imaging
Highly luminescent Ag+ nanoclusters for Hg 2+ ion detection
N-dots as a photoluminescent probe for the rapid and selective detection of Hg 2+ and Ag+ in aqueous solution
Preparation of novel fluorescent DNA bio-dots and their application for biothiols and glutathione reductase activity detection
Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for off-on sensing of Hg (II) and I-
Electrochemiluminescence of water-soluble carbon nanocrystals released electrochemically from graphite
Ag nanoparticle-decorated graphene quantum dots for label-free, rapid and sensitive detection of Ag+ and biothiols
Carbon dots as fluorescent probes for off–on detection of Cu 2+ and l-cysteine in aqueous solution
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Modulated chemical doping of individual carbon nanotubes
Rapid determination of malachite green in water and fish using a fluorescent probe based on CdTe quantum dots coated with molecularly imprinted polymer
High performance photoluminescent carbon dots for in vitro and in vivo bioimaging: effect of nitrogen doping ratios
Nitrogen-doped, thiol-functionalized carbon dots for ultrasensitive Hg (II) detection
Synthesis and unique photoluminescence properties of nitrogen-rich quantum dots and their applications
A versatile, ultralight, nitrogen-doped graphene framework
One-step preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots from oxidized debris of graphene oxide
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive, label-free detection of Fe (III) in aqueous media
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Deep ultraviolet to near-infrared emission and photoresponse in layered n-doped graphene quantum dots
Carbon dots prepared by solid state method via citric acid and 1, 10-phenanthroline for selective and sensing detection of Fe 2+ and Fe 3+
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
A rapid fluorescence switch-on assay for glutathione detection by using carbon dots–MnO 2 nanocomposites

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Aam Iqbal received her MASTER degree from University of Balochistan Quetta, Pakistan in 2016 and Ph.D. degree from College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China in 2017. Worked as a Lecturer in University of Balochistan Quetta, Pakistan. Her research interests focus on fluorescent chemosensors (CDs).

Kanwal Iqbal received her MASTER degree from University of Balochistan Quetta, Pakistan in 2016. She is currently a Ph.D. scholar at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China.
Lige Xu She is currently a master candidate at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China.

Bo Li received his Ph.D. degree from College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China in 2017. Worked as a researcher in Qinghai Institute of Salt Lakes, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research interests focus on fluorescent chemosensors (CDs).

Deyan Gong received he B.S. degree from Xi'an University of Technology, PR China in 2014. He is currently a master candidate at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China. His research interests focus on developing fluorescent chemosensors.

Yali Guo received her B.S. degree from Lanzhou University, PR China, in 2013. She is currently a master candidate at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China. Her research interests focus on developing fluorescent chemosensors.

Weisheng Liu received his Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University, PR China, in 1996. He is currently a professor at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China. His research interests focus on developing function materials and fluorescent chemosensors.

Wenwu Qin received his Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University, PR China, in 2003. He is currently a professor at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, PR China. His research interests focus on developing fluorescent chemosensors.
